Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.

Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

index 153d394..4d4afdc 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -394,6 +394,24 @@ actually resumes (the D32 guard) — is validated end to end by the privileged n
 `TestDNSHubResolveAndReroute` (Q36), with a hermetic in-namespace UDP DNS responder as
 the sole answer source (no external DNS egress).
 
+**Boot-time forced initiation (D37/T120).** A THIRD, unrelated mechanism also lives in
+`failover.go`: `startFirstPathUpHandshake`, wired in `device.go`'s `up()` for the edge
+role only (a no-op for the concentrator, which is the responder to every edge and
+initiates nothing). It is **not** part of hub failover or re-resolution — it fires
+**once**, at most, per tunnel lifetime, on the bind's `Multipath.SetOnFirstPathUp`
+latch (T117), regardless of whether the peer's endpoint list has one entry or many.
+The problem it addresses: the engine's own boot-time handshake initiation can race
+`bind.Multipath.Open` — issued before any path telemetry exists, it may hit
+`bind: no healthy path` and get dropped, yet the engine still stamps
+`peer.lastSentHandshake`, so a bare retry moments later can be silently suppressed by
+the engine's own `RekeyTimeout` guard, leaving the tunnel waiting out that ~5 s
+retransmit timer instead of re-initiating the instant a path is actually usable. The
+callback reuses the `deviceRehandshake` pattern (`ExpireCurrentKeypairs` backdates
+`lastSentHandshake` so the immediately-following `SendHandshakeInitiation` is never
+suppressed; a no-op on a cold boot with no keypairs yet). It **must** be registered
+before the probe loop starts (the latch's edge is not retroactive), so `up()` wires it
+right after the engine is constructed, well before `StartProbeLoop`.
+
 ### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`
 
 Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE

+// startFirstPathUpHandshake wires the bind's first-path-up latch (T117, Multipath.SetOnFirstPathUp)
+// to a forced WG handshake initiation against the edge's single concentrator peer (D37/T120).
+//
+// Motivation: amneziawg-go's own boot-time initiation can race the bind's Open — the FIRST
+// SendHandshakeInitiation, issued before any path telemetry exists, may hit "no healthy path"
+// (bind: ErrNoHealthyPath, the D37 symptom) and get dropped, yet the engine still stamps
+// peer.lastSentHandshake. A bare retry shortly after can then be silently suppressed by the
+// engine's RekeyTimeout guard, leaving the tunnel to wait out the engine's own ~5s retransmit
+// timer instead of re-initiating the moment a path is actually usable. rh reuses the
+// deviceRehandshake pattern: ExpireCurrentKeypairs backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
+// following initiation is never suppressed by that guard; on a cold boot with no keypairs yet it
+// is a no-op, so this only ever helps, never disrupts, an established session.
+//
+// The caller MUST register this before mp.StartProbeLoop can flip any path Up: the callback's
+// edge is NOT retroactive (T117 — SetOnFirstPathUp does not fire for an edge that already
+// happened), so registering it any later could race the very first probe response and silently
+// miss the one moment this exists to catch.
+//
+// rh is the injected rehandshake collaborator (mirroring newHubFailoverFromSpecs's rehandshake
+// parameter) so a unit test can substitute a counter for deviceRehandshake — this function itself
+// needs no *awgdevice.Device at all, only mp and cfg.Role. It is a no-op for the concentrator
+// role: the concentrator is the responder to every edge and initiates nothing
+// (startFailoverAndResolution's concentrator no-op stays untouched).
+func startFirstPathUpHandshake(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, rh rehandshake) {
+	if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge {
+		return
+	}
+	mp.SetOnFirstPathUp(rh)
+}

@@ -0,0 +1,154 @@
+package device
+
+import (
+	"crypto/rand"
+	"encoding/hex"
+	"fmt"
+	"net"
+	"net/netip"
+	"strings"
+	"sync/atomic"
+	"testing"
+	"time"
+
+	awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
+	"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun/tuntest"
+
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
+)
+
+// newFirstPathUpHarness builds a REAL, probing bind.Multipath for cfg's single loopback path,
+// brings up a REAL engine over it (so the engine's own receive routines pump the bind's
+// ReceiveFuncs — nothing here hand-rolls the receive pump), and starts a REAL loopback UDP
+// "concentrator" that reflects PROBE frames exactly as a live hub's Reflector would. This drives
+// the path to genuine telemetry.StateUp and fires the bind's T117 first-path-up latch for real,
+// so startFirstPathUpHandshake's wiring is exercised end to end without needing a live WG peer at
+// the other end of a real network link. The engine's own crypto peer (private/public key pair) is
+// unrelated to cfg's peer — startFirstPathUpHandshake only reads cfg.Role, the injected rh
+// collaborator stands in for deviceRehandshake, so no engine-peer coupling is needed here.
+func newFirstPathUpHarness(t *testing.T, cfg *config.Config) *bind.Multipath {
+	t.Helper()
+	lg := discardLogger(t)
+	psk := cfg.PeerIdentities()[0].PSK
+
+	conc, err := net.ListenUDP("udp", &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IPv4(127, 0, 0, 1)})
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("listen concentrator socket: %v", err)
+	}
+	t.Cleanup(func() { _ = conc.Close() })
+	concAP := netip.MustParseAddrPort(conc.LocalAddr().String())
+
+	reflector := telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader)
+	go func() {
+		buf := make([]byte, 4096)
+		for {
+			n, from, rerr := conc.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(buf)
+			if rerr != nil {
+				return
+			}
+			echo, _, eerr := reflector.Reflect(append([]byte(nil), buf[:n]...))
+			if eerr != nil {
+				continue // not an authenticatable probe (e.g. a stray WG handshake init) — ignore
+			}
+			_, _ = conc.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, from)
+		}
+	}()
+

113	// resolves to at least one address and EVERY resolved address is loopback — a

115	// binds every interface and is refused.
116	func requireLoopback(addr string) error {
117		host, _, err := net.SplitHostPort(addr)

120		}
121		if host == "" {

126			if !ip.IsLoopback() {

128			}
129			return nil
130		}

132		// Hostname: resolve and require every resolved address to be loopback.
133		ips, err := net.LookupIP(host)
134		if err != nil {
135			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: cannot resolve listen host %q: %w", host, err)
136		}
137		if len(ips) == 0 {
138			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: listen host %q resolved to no addresses", host)
139		}
140		for _, ip := range ips {
141			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
142				return fmt.Errorf("%w: host %q resolves to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, host, ip)
143			}
144		}
145		return nil
146	}

151	// resolves a hostname listen address itself — a second, independent lookup from

153	// that a resolver cannot subvert. A non-TCP or non-loopback bound address
154	// yields ErrNonLoopbackBind so the caller can close the listener and refuse.
155	func verifyLoopbackBind(a net.Addr) error {
156		tcp, ok := a.(*net.TCPAddr)
157		if !ok {
158			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-TCP address %v", ErrNonLoopbackBind, a)
159		}
160		if !tcp.IP.IsLoopback() {
161			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, tcp.IP)
162		}
163		return nil
164	}

112	// must satisfy IsLoopback; a hostname (e.g. "localhost") is accepted only if it
113	// resolves to at least one address and EVERY resolved address is loopback — a

115	// binds every interface and is refused.
116	func requireLoopback(addr string) error {
117		host, _, err := net.SplitHostPort(addr)
118		if err != nil {
119			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: invalid listen address %q: %w", addr, err)
120		}
121		if host == "" {

123		}

126			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
127				return fmt.Errorf("%w: got %q", ErrNonLoopbackBind, addr)
128			}
129			return nil
130		}
131	
132		// Hostname: resolve and require every resolved address to be loopback.
133		ips, err := net.LookupIP(host)
134		if err != nil {
135			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: cannot resolve listen host %q: %w", host, err)
136		}
137		if len(ips) == 0 {
138			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: listen host %q resolved to no addresses", host)
139		}
140		for _, ip := range ips {
141			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
142				return fmt.Errorf("%w: host %q resolves to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, host, ip)
143			}
144		}
145		return nil
146	}

149	// guard: it asserts that the address the kernel ACTUALLY bound (ln.Addr()) is a
150	// loopback TCP address, independent of any DNS resolution. Because net.Listen
151	// resolves a hostname listen address itself — a second, independent lookup from
152	// requireLoopback's — verifying the concrete bound address is the only check
153	// that a resolver cannot subvert. A non-TCP or non-loopback bound address
154	// yields ErrNonLoopbackBind so the caller can close the listener and refuse.
155	func verifyLoopbackBind(a net.Addr) error {
156		tcp, ok := a.(*net.TCPAddr)
157		if !ok {
158			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-TCP address %v", ErrNonLoopbackBind, a)
159		}
160		if !tcp.IP.IsLoopback() {
161			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, tcp.IP)
162		}
163		return nil
164	}

Let me confirm the production ordering is fully sound: the callback (real `deviceRehandshake`) does `dev.LookupPeer` which no-ops on a missing peer — the engine peer is added by `IpcSet` (after registration) but still before `StartProbeLoop`, so by the time any path can reach Up and fire the callback, the peer exists. The ordering is correct on both ends.

Summary of evidence gathered:

1. **Ordering (registered before probing)** — confirmed. `startFirstPathUpHandshake` is called at `device.go:329`, immediately after `awgdevice.NewDevice`, well before `IpcSet`/`dev.Up`/`StartProbeLoop`. The T117 latch is genuinely one-shot and non-retroactive (`multipath.go:1733` stores the fn; the edge is never replayed), so early registration is required and satisfied.

2. **Fires exactly once for edge, never for concentrator** — confirmed by both the CAS-gated latch flip (`multipath.go:1814`, `everUp.CompareAndSwap(false,true)`, sticky, fired via `go (*cb)()` off the receive path) and the two passing tests driving a real reflector/prober to genuine `StateUp`. The concentrator test guards its negative with an `EverHadLivePath()` precondition, so it is not vacuous.

3. **`deviceRehandshake` reuse** — correct. `ExpireCurrentKeypairs` backdates `lastSentHandshake` so the following `SendHandshakeInitiation` escapes the RekeyTimeout guard; `LookupPeer==nil` and cold-boot-no-keypairs both no-op. Reused verbatim from the already-validated hub-failover path.

4. **Concentrator noop untouched** — confirmed. `startFailoverAndResolution` is byte-identical; the new `startFirstPathUpHandshake` early-returns for `cfg.Role != RoleEdge`.

5. **`Peers[0]` safety** — the new `deviceRehandshake(dev, cfg.WireGuard.Peers[0].PublicKey)` is evaluated unconditionally in `up()` for both roles, but config validation (`config.go:1080`) rejects zero-peer configs, and `ids[0]` is already indexed unconditionally pre-T120, so no index-out-of-range risk.

6. **Checks green** — `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, `go test ./internal/device/... ./internal/bind/...`, `go test -race -count=2 ./internal/device/...` (7.1s, clean), and full `go test ./internal/...` all pass. The test validates wiring via an injected counter (not an end-to-end WG handshake), but that is exactly what the acceptance criterion specifies, and the underlying `deviceRehandshake` is pre-existing validated code. No defects, no ambiguities.
